## reference · emotional design delight desirability microinteraction feedback trust credibility beyond usable
* User delight refers to any positive emotional affect that a user may have when interacting with a device or interface.
* Delight can be categorized into two types: surface delight and deep delight.
* Surface delight is local and contextual, derived from isolated interface features such as animations, microcopy, and sound interactions.
* Deep delight is holistic, achieved when all user needs are met, including functionality, reliability, usability, and pleasurability.
* A product can be delightful only if it is usable.
* Functionality, reliability, and usability are foundational elements that must be met before pursuing delight.
* Streamlined workflows and reduced pain points can help achieve deep delight.
* Examples of sites that achieve deep delight include Yelp.com and Unroll.me, which provide functional, reliable, and usable experiences with pleasurable interactions.
* Due to negativity bias, users remember bad experiences more than good ones, so delightful features may fail to provide sustainable benefits if basic functionality and reliability are lacking.
* Usability issues can make delightful features fall flat, as seen in examples like The Clove Club and Ithaca Bakery's websites.
* Prioritizing usability is crucial, as it is half the battle in creating a delightful experience.
Sources: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/theory-user-delight/
